title: What is Upsampling?
concept: Upsampling takes a small feature map and makes it bigger again — the opposite of what convolutions and pooling do — which matters for tasks like segmentation where the output must match the input's resolution.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a grouped convolution do to an input's channels before applying separate filters?

options.text: Splits them into separate groups, each processed independently by its own filters
Merges all channels into a single value before any filtering
Discards half of the channels at random

exercises.explain: Exactly — grouped convolutions split channels into independent groups, each with its own filters and no cross-group mixing. Now let's flip convolutions on their head: what if you needed to make a feature map BIGGER instead of smaller?

cards.title: Everything so far has been shrinking
cards.body: Every convolution trick you've learned this whole deep dive — dilation, grouping, striding — still moves in one direction: a big feature map comes in, a smaller (or same-size) one comes out. But what happens when a task needs the exact opposite?

cards.title: What is upsampling?
cards.body: Upsampling means increasing the spatial size of a feature map — turning something like a 4x4 grid of numbers into an 8x8 grid, or bigger. It's the mirror image of what strided convolutions and pooling do: instead of shrinking height and width, upsampling grows them back.

cards.title: Blowing up a thumbnail
cards.body: Think of stretching a small thumbnail image out to fill a big screen. The thumbnail didn't gain new information — it got spread across more pixels. Upsampling does the same thing to a feature map: more grid cells, filled in from the smaller original.

cards.title: Why segmentation needs it
cards.body: A medical scan segmentation model needs to label every single pixel of a 256x256 image as 'tumor' or 'not tumor.' But its convolution layers shrink that image down to a compact 8x8 feature map to find patterns efficiently. Something has to blow that 8x8 map back up to 256x256 before the model can output a full-size mask — that's upsampling's job.

cards.title: Upsampling doesn't recover lost detail
cards.body: It's tempting to think upsampling 'undoes' the shrinking and restores the original image perfectly. It can't — once pooling or a strided conv throws detail away, that detail is gone. Upsampling only makes the grid bigger; it can't magically reconstruct information that was never kept.

cards.title: Downsampling vs. upsampling
cards.body: Two opposite moves in a CNN:

right.text:
Grows height & width
fills in new grid cells
e.g. 4x4 → 8x8

exercises.prompt: What does upsampling do to a feature map's spatial size?
exercises.explain: Right — upsampling grows the spatial grid (height and width), the opposite of pooling or a strided convolution.

exercises.prompt: True or false: upsampling can perfectly recover the exact detail that pooling or a strided convolution discarded earlier in the network.
exercises.explain: False — upsampling only makes the grid bigger. Detail thrown away during downsampling is gone for good; it can't be magically reconstructed.

exercises.sentence: Upsampling is the opposite of pooling and strided convolutions — instead of shrinking a feature map, it makes it ___.
exercises.explain: Right — upsampling grows a feature map's spatial size instead of shrinking it.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly describes a use for upsampling?

options.text: A segmentation model needs its final output mask to match the original 256x256 input resolution, so a small 8x8 feature map is grown back up
A classifier needs to shrink a 224x224 image down to a single label
A 1x1 conv mixes channel information without changing spatial size

exercises.explain: Exactly — matching a small feature map back up to the input's resolution is exactly what upsampling is for.

exercises.prompt: A network's convolution and pooling layers shrink a 128x128 input image down to a 16x16 feature map by the time it reaches the last conv layer. The task is semantic segmentation, which requires a per-pixel output the same size as the input. What must happen to the 16x16 feature map, and why?

options.text: It must be upsampled back up to 128x128, because the output mask needs to align with every original input pixel
Nothing — segmentation models output a single label per image, not a full mask
It must be shrunk further, since segmentation only needs a compact summary
It must be duplicated 16 times side by side without resizing

exercises.explain: Exactly — the 16x16 map must grow back to 128x128 so every original pixel gets a label. Coming up next: simple upsampling follows one fixed rule — what if the network could learn the best way to expand instead?
